planting.note: Root stem tip cuttings under high humidity or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, strongly acidic to slightly acidic, humus-rich, orchid-bark-amended soil in bright indirect light or partial shade after frost; medinilla requires high humidity (above 60%), consistent warmth, and an orchid-like open, free-draining substrate — standard potting soil compacted around its roots causes root rot and decline.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, humus-rich, acidic, bark-amended soil in bright indirect light or partial shade; Malaysian orchid (rose grape, showy medinilla) is a Philippine tropical epiphyte of the family Melastomataceae — one of the most spectacular flowering plants in the world — that produces enormous, pendant, bougainvillea-like flower panicles composed of clusters of small, deep-rose-pink to magenta flowers hanging from pale-pink to rose-red, waxy bracts, dangling in dramatic, chandelier-like cascades from the tips of the stiff, upright, square stems with large, sessile, dark-green, prominently veined leaves. The entire flower structure is breathtakingly ornamental and can reach 12–18 inches in length on established specimens. As an epiphyte, medinilla naturally grows on tree branches in the Philippine rainforest and is adapted to excellent drainage, high humidity, and warm, bright-shade conditions — it requires an open, orchid-like substrate and consistent humidity rather than consistently moist soil. In zone 10–12 it can be grown outdoors in bright shade; elsewhere it is a challenging but rewarding conservatory or greenhouse specimen.

planting.note: Divide basal offshoots or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade to full shade after frost; tree philodendron becomes very large over time — a mature specimen can spread 6–10 feet — and its massive leaf stalks and trunk-like base require adequate space, so site with its eventual size in mind.

directions: Divide basal offshoots or plant container specimens in well-drained, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade; tree philodendron (lacy tree philodendron, selloum, horsehead philodendron) is a South American tropical perennial that is among the most architecturally dramatic shade plants available for zone 9–12 gardens — a non-climbing, self-heading philodendron that develops a trunk-like stem and spreads outward from its center with enormous, deeply divided, glossy, dark-green, pinnately lobed leaves that can reach 3 feet across on mature specimens, creating a bold, sculptural, rainforest-understory effect. Now correctly classified as Thaumatophyllum bipinnatifidum, it was long known as Philodendron bipinnatifidum or Philodendron selloum in horticulture. In zone 9 it is often frozen back but regrows vigorously from the roots; in zone 10–12 it is a permanent, year-round architectural specimen. It develops aerial roots that anchor it and absorb moisture. The large, tropical form and remarkable leaf shape make it a definitive specimen plant for shaded tropical-style gardens and large container plantings.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade after frost; torch ginger demands rich, moist, organic soil and regular fertilization — in poor or dry soil it produces few flowers and its growth is stunted; top-dress with compost annually and fertilize monthly with a balanced fertilizer during the growing season.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in consistently moist, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade; torch ginger (red ginger lily, Philippine wax flower, bunga kantan) is a Southeast Asian tropical perennial that produces one of the most dramatic and architecturally striking flower structures of any plant in the tropical garden — the individual flowers emerge directly from the ground on separate, erect stems distinct from the leafy shoots, opening into large, ornate, waxy, pink-to-red, cone-shaped, artichoke-like flower heads composed of tightly overlapping, waxy, pink or red petals arranged in a perfect geometric spiral, rising above the garden floor at the end of stout, 3–5-foot stalks as solitary flame-like torches. The entire structure has an almost architectural, sculptural precision. The large, lance-shaped, deep-green leaves on the vegetative stems rise to 8–15 feet. In addition to its ornamental value, the flower buds and young shoots are used in Southeast Asian cuisine (Thai, Malaysian, and Indonesian cooking) as an aromatic flavoring ingredient. Requires genuinely tropical conditions (zone 10–12) and rich, moist organic soil. An essential large-scale tropical specimen.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or water, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich potting mix in partial shade; aluminum plant prefers consistently moist but never waterlogged soil — it is sensitive to drought (leaves collapse quickly) but equally sensitive to root rot in compacted or consistently wet media.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade; aluminum plant is a Vietnamese shade perennial of the nettle family (Urticaceae) — one of the most easily grown and most instantly recognizable small tropical foliage plants — whose oval, quilted, dark-green leaves are distinctively marked with four elevated, raised, silver-white, metallic panels between the leaf veins that catch light and create a three-dimensional, embossed, hammered-aluminum effect on the leaf surface. The silver patterning is so striking and consistent that the plant is immediately recognizable even at a distance. It grows as a compact, bushy, somewhat succulent-stemmed mound and is among the most tolerant of lower indoor light levels of any tropical foliage plant, thriving in bright-shade to moderate-shade conditions where many other variegated plants would lose their markings. As an outdoor plant in zone 10–12, it forms a low mounding groundcover in shade gardens. Tip-pinch regularly to maintain compact, bushy growth — plants left unpinched become tall and lanky within a few months. Propagates trivially easily from stem cuttings rooted in water.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun after frost; wrightia is one of the classic bonsai subjects of Southeast Asia and its naturally fine-twiggy branching pattern, small leaves, and appealing flower production make it ideal for bonsai training — it tolerates aggressive pruning and responds with dense, profuse new growth.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; water jasmine (sacred Buddhist tree, wild water plum) is a Southeast Asian tropical evergreen shrub widely cultivated throughout the tropical world — particularly in Thailand, Cambodia, and Malaysia — both for its ornamental beauty and its deep spiritual significance, as it is traditionally planted at temples and sacred sites, and its dangling clusters of small, pure-white, five-petaled, star-shaped flowers with long filamentous corona lobes are used as Buddhist temple offerings. The flowers are produced continuously and profusely throughout the warm season on the drooping, fine-twiggy branches, with a sweet, delicate fragrance, creating a graceful, weeping, white-flowered effect. The plant belongs to the Apocynaceae (oleander family) and exudes a milky sap when cut; all parts are toxic if ingested. In addition to its garden use as a specimen and informal flowering hedge, wrightia is one of the preeminent tropical bonsai subjects, widely grown in competitive Southeast Asian bonsai culture. Excellent in containers and as a specimen in zone 10–12.
